question:
Research evidence-based UI/UX design for a product-status dashboard built specifically for a solo software developer who has ADHD. The dashboard is a "Where am I · is it really fixed?" surface that shows, across several of his software products, each product's current state and — critically — distinguishes an author's CLAIM of "done/verified" from machine-VERIFIED done (proof-backed). Today it renders as a spartan, self-contained (stdlib-only, no JS framework) static HTML page with labeled status "bars" per item: verified, mismatch (claimed-done but not proof-backed), focus (a single at-most-one "work on this next" pointer), work-in-progress column, and derived evidence label; text currently runs together with minimal visual hierarchy.

Cover four things and make the output ACTIONABLE for this specific dashboard:
1) Evidence-based ADHD-friendly UI/UX principles: reducing cognitive load and overwhelm, offloading working memory, attention/salience and visual hierarchy, executive-function support (task initiation, "what do I do next"), single-focus / one-thing-at-a-time, momentum/progress cues, and avoiding notification/alert fatigue. Cite research where possible.
2) Concrete design patterns that implement those principles: color and status semantics (and accessibility / not relying on color alone), information chunking and hierarchy, glanceability, trust/verification signals that clearly separate "claimed" from "verified," and calm/low-stimulation vs. dopamine/reward design trade-offs.
3) Examples of similar/comparable products and what their UI/UX gets right or wrong for ADHD users: ADHD-oriented productivity/task apps (e.g. Sunsama, Amazing Marvin, Akiflow, Tiimo, Todoist), project/status tools (Linear, Trello/kanban, GitHub Projects), and developer/CI status dashboards. Pull concrete, specific patterns, not generic praise.
4) A concrete recommendation set mapped to our dashboard: how to restructure the current bars/cards/focus-pointer/verified-vs-mismatch rendering to genuinely help an ADHD user answer "where am I and what's really done vs just claimed," while ideally staying compatible with a lightweight self-contained HTML output (note where a richer stack would be needed and whether it's worth it). The overarching goal is a dashboard that reduces overwhelm and makes the next action obvious.
summary: For a solo ADHD developer's "where am I / is it really fixed?" dashboard, the research converges on one overriding directive: minimize the attention and working-memory the surface demands, because ADHD carries a chronic, well-replicated working-memory deficit (effect sizes ~0.43–1.06) and neurotypical working memory itself holds only ~3–4 items at once. The dashboard should therefore act as a cognitive-offloading device (externalizing state so nothing must be held in the head), chunk content into single-concept cards with strong visual hierarchy, and present a single obvious next action rather than everything at once (progressive disclosure). The claimed-vs-verified distinction is itself a first-class design pattern with a real-world precedent: Linear deliberately refuses to auto-complete a project even when all issues are done, forcing a manual "is it truly done?" confirmation — exactly the claimed/verified gap this dashboard surfaces. Status must be encoded redundantly (color plus text/icon/shape), never color alone, because ~8% of men have color-vision deficiency and screen readers do not announce color. Calm-technology principles (require the smallest possible attention, inform-and-create-calm, use the periphery) argue for a low-stimulation surface where stable/verified states rest quietly in the periphery while the one focus item and any claimed-but-unverified "mismatch" command the center.

findings.claim: ADHD involves a chronic, heavily-replicated working-memory deficit, and neurotypical working memory holds only ~3-4 discrete items at once — so the dashboard must not require the user to hold multiple product states in mind, and should act as an external cognitive-offloading device (lists/labels/checklists that externalize state).
findings.evidence: ADHD working-memory deficit is one of the most robust replicated findings (Martinussen 2005 meta-analysis, between-group ES 0.43-1.06, independent of general IQ; Rapport/Kofler: ADHD attention deficits driven by exceeding storage/rehearsal capacity at LOWER cognitive loads). Neurotypical WM capacity is ~3-4 chunks (Cowan's 'magical number four', 2001/2010; 2024 Journal of Cognition modelling review confirms 4). Cognitive offloading — transferring a task's processing requirements onto the environment via lists/calendars/checklists — is an established compensatory construct (Risko & Gilbert 2016, Trends in Cognitive Sciences) that improves memory-task performance (PMC6942100) and is standard OT/clinical practice for ADHD (PMC8334010). Design inference: the dashboard's core value is that the user should never have to remember which product is where — the screen IS the working memory.
sources: Martinussen et al. 2005 J Am Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry 44:377-384
Cowan 2001 Behavioral and Brain Sciences; Cowan 2010 Current Directions
Risko & Gilbert 2016 Trends in Cognitive Sciences

findings.claim: High cognitive load specifically impairs focus, memory-of-steps, and task completion for ADHD users; extraneous load from clutter and decorative/non-essential UI directly worsens overload — so reducing on-screen density and stripping non-load-bearing elements is a primary design goal for this dashboard.
findings.evidence: Cognitive Load Theory (Sweller 1988): working memory has limited capacity and is overwhelmed when too many elements compete; extraneous load is load imposed by presentation design not serving the task. ADHD-specific: high cognitive load makes it hard to focus, remember steps, or complete tasks (corroborated by Le Cunff 2024 scoping review, Eur J Neurosci 10.1111/ejn.16201; PMC6996017; PubMed 24411642). Mayer's Coherence Principle (excluding extraneous material) held in 23/23 experiments, median effect size 0.86; seductive-details effect shows interesting-but-irrelevant decoration actively harms performance. For the dashboard: remove decorative chrome, keep only status-bearing elements, and cut on-screen density — every non-essential element taxes an already-constrained system.
sources: Mayer Coherence Principle; Harp & Mayer (seductive details)

findings.claim: Present one thing at a time: chunk content into single-concept cards, use progressive disclosure (reveal detail only when needed), and break complex work into small sequential steps with visible progress — presenting everything at once overwhelms ADHD users.
findings.evidence: Multiple converging claims: chunking into small sections with clear headings/bulleted lists reduces load (Understood.org, Pearson, CLT); progressive disclosure — revealing only what's relevant in the moment — supports limited working memory (grounded in CLT + Miller's Law, NN/g '4 Principles to Reduce Cognitive Load'); complex tasks broken into step-by-step chunks meaningfully reduce load (CDC, NHS, Understood; PMC11870651 'adaptive chunking improves effective WM capacity'); each CARD should communicate a single concept ('One card, one concept' — Material Design: a card 'can't merge with another card or split into multiple cards'; NN/g cards component). IMPORTANT ACTIONABLE CAVEAT: NN/g warns that sequential WIZARDS that HIDE prior context can INCREASE cognitive load and cause context-loss for ADHD users — so prefer visible-progress chunking (all steps visible with a progress cue) over hidden multi-step flows. For the dashboard: one product per card, one concept per card, and expand item detail on demand rather than showing all evidence text at once.
sources: Material Design cards guidance; NN/g cards + wizards guidance

findings.claim: Establish a strong, deliberate visual hierarchy so the eye moves from most-important to least-important automatically — use typography sizing/placement, strong RELATIVE contrast, and generous spacing to aid glanceability — but avoid harsh maximum contrast (pure #000 on #FFF).
findings.evidence: A well-designed card establishes hierarchy so 'the eye moves from most important to least important without thinking about it' (NN/g, Gestalt, Material Design). Reducing visual/cognitive overload via simplified layouts, limited competing elements, and clear hierarchy is a primary principle for preventing neurodivergent overwhelm (backed by W3C COGA cognitive-accessibility guidance; accessibilitychecker.org: 'clear visual hierarchy is vital for neurodivergent users who may struggle with information overload'). Strong contrast + proper spacing enhance hierarchy and scanning/glanceability. ACTIONABLE CAVEAT (from the contrast finding's verifier): harsh MAXIMUM contrast (pure black on pure white) causes glare/visual stress for dyslexic/Irlen readers (British Dyslexia Association) — use strong RELATIVE contrast between hierarchy levels (focus/mismatch/verified vs. body) plus generous whitespace, but prefer near-black text on an off-white/soft surface. For the dashboard: the current 'text running together with minimal hierarchy' is the core failure to fix — size and weight the status label and product name far above the evidence text.
sources: W3C COGA guidance; NN/g; Material Design

findings.claim: Status must be encoded REDUNDANTLY — color paired with text labels, icons, patterns and/or shapes — never color alone, because ~8% of men (and 0.5% of women) have color-vision deficiency and screen readers do not announce color.
findings.evidence: Canonical prevalence: ~8% of men / 0.5% of women have color-vision deficiency (Colour Blind Awareness, VisionCenter, NCBI/PMC all corroborate 'up to 8% of males, 0.5% of females'). Color-only status therefore fails a meaningful share of users. WCAG 2.x SC 1.4.1 'Use of Color' (W3C WAI, current through 2.2 in 2026), reinforced by Section508.gov and the U.S. Web Design System's alert/step-indicator components, requires color not be the ONLY visual means of conveying information — pair with text/icon/shape. Screen readers do not announce color by default, so color-only conveys nothing to those users. DIRECTLY ACTIONABLE for verified/mismatch/focus semantics: never rely on green=verified, red/amber=mismatch alone — each state needs an explicit text label AND a distinguishing icon/shape (e.g. checkmark for verified, warning glyph for claimed-but-unverified mismatch).

findings.claim: The claimed-vs-verified distinction is a legitimate, precedented design pattern: Linear deliberately does NOT auto-mark a project complete even when all its issues are done — status must be manually confirmed — validating the dashboard's core act of separating an author's CLAIM of done from machine-VERIFIED done.
findings.evidence: Linear's own canonical docs (linear.app/docs/project-status): 'Project statuses are updated manually—we do not do this automatically, even if all issues are completed.' Rationale from Linear: 'Some projects have post-launch tasks or documentation work that does not live in Linear. The manual step ensures you confirm the project is truly done.' This is a deliberate design choice by a leading, ADHD-popular project tool that separates task-completion from formal 'truly done' reporting — exactly the claimed/verified gap this dashboard exists to surface. It validates treating 'claimed done' and 'verified done' as two distinct, visually-distinct states rather than collapsing them. For the dashboard: the 'mismatch' state (claimed-done but not proof-backed) is the highest-value signal and should be visually loudest after the focus pointer.

findings.claim: Give every item clear, visible completion states plus start/end points and progress/momentum cues — visible completion reduces task-completion anxiety and supports executive function (task initiation, 'what do I do next').
findings.evidence: Tasks should have clear visible start/end points and completion states to reduce task-completion anxiety (rooted in Nielsen's 'visibility of system status' heuristic + the Zeigarnik effect: incomplete tasks generate intrusive mental tension). Complex tasks should be broken into small steps WITH progress indicators and micro-flows to support executive-function challenges (NICE guidance recommends structuring goals into smaller achievable actions; ADHD task-initiation research advises 'extremely small, concrete steps requiring minimal decision-making'; visible progress/checklist completion triggers dopamine reward that sustains motivation; 2025 Frontiers in Psychiatry: stepwise goal-setting improved functioning in inattentive ADHD). For the dashboard: the WIP column should decompose items into visible steps with a per-product progress/momentum cue, and the single at-most-one 'focus' pointer directly serves task-initiation by removing the 'what do I do next' decision.
sources: Nielsen 'visibility of system status'; Zeigarnik effect

findings.claim: Adopt calm-technology principles: the surface should require the smallest possible amount of attention, inform-and-create-calm (avoid alert fatigue), and use the periphery — letting stable/verified states rest quietly while only the single focus item and any 'mismatch' escalate to the center.
findings.evidence: Calm Tech Institute's principles (from Weiser & Brown, Xerox PARC; codified by Amber Case): Principle 1 'Require the smallest possible amount of attention' (treat attention as finite); Principle 2 'Inform and create calm' (deliver information without stress/alert fatigue); Principle 3 'Make use of the periphery' + Principle 5 'Communicate without speaking' (convey status ambiently rather than demanding foreground focus). CRITICAL APPLICATION NUANCE (from verifier): calm tech emphasizes fluid movement between periphery AND center — a signal must escalate to the foreground when action is needed, and ambient/peripheral treatment suits NON-critical info. Since this dashboard's core job (the focus pointer, mismatch alerts, 'is it really fixed?') IS action-demanding, apply calm treatment to STABLE/verified states (quiet, low-saturation, peripheral) while the one focus item and mismatches command center attention. This also directly addresses avoiding notification/alert fatigue.
sources: Weiser & Brown (Xerox PARC); Amber Case, 'Designing Calm Technology'

findings.claim: Provide subtle confirmation feedback (micro-interactions confirming an action/state worked) to reduce anxiety — but keep it subtle to avoid alert fatigue.
findings.evidence: Subtle confirmation feedback reduces anxiety by confirming system behavior — a restatement of Nielsen's Heuristic #1 'Visibility of System Status', which NN/g explicitly nicknames the 'anti-anxiety heuristic' ('when we don't have visible system status, we have uncertainty'). Micro-interactions that instantly confirm an action 'reduce user anxiety' and cut repeated actions (NN/g microinteractions; ACM 10.1145/3452853.3452865). The word 'subtle' correctly scopes it: over-frequent/noisy feedback causes alert fatigue. For the dashboard: when a state flips from 'claimed' to machine-verified, a small, calm confirmation (not a loud alert) reassures the user the proof landed — reinforcing trust in the verified signal without adding to notification noise.
sources: NN/g 'Visibility of System Status'; NN/g microinteractions

findings.claim: SYNTHESIZED RECOMMENDATION SET for the dashboard — a concrete restructuring that stays compatible with lightweight self-contained stdlib-only HTML, with notes on where a richer stack would be needed.
findings.evidence: Mapping the evidence to the specific surface: (1) ONE FOCUS ELEMENT AT TOP: render the at-most-one focus pointer as a distinct, visually dominant banner/card above everything else — it removes the 'what do I do next' decision (task-initiation) and is pure CSS. (2) THREE-TIER HIERARCHY BY ATTENTION: focus (center, loud) > mismatch/claimed-but-unverified (loud, warning glyph + label) > verified (quiet, low-saturation, peripheral/collapsed) — implements calm-tech periphery + reduces load; pure CSS. (3) ONE CARD PER PRODUCT, ONE CONCEPT: fix the 'text running together' problem by giving each card a sized/weighted status LABEL + product name far above the derived-evidence text; generous spacing, near-black-on-off-white not pure contrast. (4) REDUNDANT STATUS ENCODING: every state = text label + icon/shape + color (checkmark=verified, warning=mismatch), never color alone — WCAG 1.4.1; trivially static HTML. (5) PROGRESSIVE DISCLOSURE of evidence: show the derived-evidence label collapsed by default, expand on demand — achievable with native HTML <details>/<summary>, no JS framework. (6) VISIBLE PROGRESS in the WIP column with a per-item momentum cue (e.g., steps done / total) — static HTML/CSS. (7) CLAIMED-vs-VERIFIED as the loudest distinction (Linear precedent) — a proof-backed 'verified' badge vs. a distinct 'claimed, unproven' mismatch badge. WHERE A RICHER STACK IS NEEDED (and whether worth it): live auto-refresh of proof status, subtle confirmation micro-animations on state-flip, and any interactive filtering require JS. Given the ADHD goals, native <details> + CSS covers 90% of the value; a small vanilla-JS sprinkle (no framework) for a quiet state-flip confirmation is worth it, but a full JS framework is NOT justified — it would add build complexity against a surface whose whole point is calm minimalism.

caveats: SOURCE QUALITY: The ADHD-specific UX design guidance (welcomingweb, aufaitux, accessibilitychecker, the Medium '16 principles' post, stan.vision, accessibility.chat, ot4adhd) are all blog/agency-quality secondary sources. They survived adversarial verification only because each restates a NON-extraordinary, well-established principle that is independently backed by strong primary literature (Sweller's Cognitive Load Theory, Cowan's working-memory capacity, Martinussen's ADHD WM meta-analysis, Risko & Gilbert on cognitive offloading, WCAG, NN/g heuristics). Treat the blogs as pointers to consensus, not as the evidence itself. VENDOR SELF-DESCRIPTION: The Linear and Calm Tech Institute claims come from the vendors' own docs — appropriate for descriptive claims about their own products/frameworks, but the Calm Tech Institute has a commercial certification arm (the principles themselves trace to Weiser/Brown/Case, which is sound). MECHANISM UNCERTAINTY: One 2022 fNIRS study reframes adult ADHD as a 'cognitive state disorder rather than a working-memory deficit' — this qualifies the MECHANISM but not the design effect (reduce overwhelm) which holds regardless. TWO REFUTED CLAIMS (transparency): a claim that 'overly bright colors/animations/clutter cause discomfort so calm low-stimulation is preferable' and a claim tying executive-function support to WCAG 2.2 AA 1.4.3 specifically both failed the vote (1-2) and are NOT relied upon — though the calm-technology finding covers similar ground from a stronger base. GENERALIZATION LIMIT: almost all ADHD-specific application rests on general cognitive-science + practitioner consensus rather than large ADHD-specific RCTs of dashboard UIs; there is no direct study of a 'claimed-vs-verified developer status dashboard' for ADHD users. TIME-SENSITIVITY: WCAG (current through 2.2 in 2026) and the cited product docs (Linear) can change; re-verify vendor behavior before relying on it long-term.
openQuestions: What is the optimal DEFAULT-collapsed vs. default-expanded balance for the derived-evidence text? Progressive disclosure reduces load, but for a proof-backed 'is it really fixed?' surface, hiding the proof might undermine trust in the verified signal — this trade-off is unresearched for this specific use case.

Does the ADHD 'dopamine/reward' momentum design (celebratory completion cues) genuinely help sustained motivation here, or does it conflict with the calm/low-stimulation directive? The two refuted claims touched this tension but it was not resolved by the surviving evidence.

For a SOLO user (audience of one), how much accessibility redundancy (color+icon+shape) is truly necessary versus over-engineering — i.e., does the developer himself have color-vision deficiency? The 8%-prevalence argument is population-level; the single-user optimum may differ, though redundant encoding is low-cost insurance.

Is a quiet vanilla-JS state-flip confirmation worth the departure from a pure stdlib-only static HTML output, or should the dashboard stay 100% static and rely on the user re-generating/refreshing it? The evidence supports subtle confirmation feedback but does not weigh it against the maintenance value of a zero-JS artifact.

refuted.claim: Executive-function support requires visible feedback, instant visual confirmations, and progress indicators, especially in longer flows/forms; color must not be the sole signal (WCAG 2.2 AA 1.4.3).
Overly bright colors, rapid animations, and cluttered interfaces cause discomfort or distraction for neurodivergent users, so calm low-stimulation design is preferable.
